title: Excel vs Google Sheets: Which Should You Use in 2026?
dek: Choose Google Sheets for free, real-time collaboration and anywhere access. Choose Excel for heavy data, advanced analysis, and offline power. Most people are happy with Sheets; power users still need Excel. Here is the honest breakdown.

seo.metaTitle: Excel vs Google Sheets: Which Wins in 2026?
seo.metaDescription: Excel vs Google Sheets compared on price, collaboration, power, and offline use — with a clear recommendation for each type of user.

richText: Excel and Google Sheets do the same core job — rows, columns, and formulas — but they are built around different priorities. Excel is a powerhouse for analysis; Google Sheets is built for sharing and simplicity. The right choice depends on how you actually work, not on which is "better" overall.

items: if you value free access, real-time collaboration, and working from any device.
if you handle large datasets, need advanced analysis, or work offline a lot.

body.caption: Excel vs Google Sheets at a glance
rows: Slows down on very large sheets
Power Query, Power Pivot, more functions

body.richText: For serious data work — hundreds of thousands of rows, Power Query transformations, complex modeling — Excel is still ahead. It has more built-in functions, better charting depth, and tools like Power Pivot that Sheets does not match. Finance, accounting, and analytics roles usually standardize on Excel for this reason.

For teams, Sheets is hard to beat: several people can edit the same file at once, changes save automatically, and version history is built in. It is free, runs in any browser, and connects easily to other Google tools. For most everyday spreadsheets, that convenience matters more than Excel's extra power.
You do not have to choose forever. Both can open .xlsx files, so many people draft in Sheets for collaboration and export to Excel when they need heavy analysis.

items.question: Is Google Sheets as good as Excel?
items.answerRichText: For everyday spreadsheets and teamwork, yes — many users never hit its limits. For heavy analysis, very large datasets, or advanced tools like Power Query, Excel is still more capable.

items.question: Can Google Sheets open Excel files?
items.answerRichText: Yes. Google Sheets can open and edit .xlsx files, and can export back to Excel format. Very complex Excel features (some macros or advanced formulas) may not carry over perfectly.
